Appendix: Roadway Block Variables Documentation

Updated version with corrected roadway length variable (length_m)

1. Spatial Structure

Road segments are represented as line geometries, while census tracts are represented as polygon geometries.

Context

This study integrates roadway-level data (line geometries) with census tract-level data (polygons). Each road segment is spatially joined to a census tract using its geometry.

Variables

  • Census tract = polygon
  • GEOID = Census tract identifier
  • Road segment = line (LineString)

2. Identification Variables

These variables identify roadway records and support validation.

Context

These variables uniquely identify road segments but do not describe roadway characteristics. The original SHAPELEN field is dropped because it is invalid, and a corrected roadway length variable (length_m) is computed from geometry for analytical use.

Variables

VariableDescriptionModeling / Analytical Use
ROUTEIDUnique segment identifierUsed for joins and validation; not used directly in modeling
SHAPELENOriginal segment length fieldDropped because all values appear as 0 / invalid
length_mCorrected roadway segment length in meters, computed from geometryUsed as the valid length measure for road length aggregation, exposure normalization, and density metrics

Length Calculation Method

# Step 2: Compute roadway length in meters
roadwayB_geo_m = roadwayB_geo.to_crs(epsg=3857)
roadwayB_geo["length_m"] = roadwayB_geo_m.geometry.length

Notes

  • length_m is computed after projecting roadway geometries to a meter-based coordinate reference system (EPSG:3857).
  • length_m replaces the invalid SHAPELEN field.
  • length_m is important for exposure normalization, road-length density metrics, and fair comparisons across census tracts.

3. Traffic Exposure

AADT and truck composition variables.

Context

Traffic exposure is a primary driver of crash risk. Higher traffic volumes generally lead to more crash opportunities. AADT is modeled rather than directly observed, so it may introduce estimation bias.

Variables

  • AADT: Average daily traffic volume; core exposure variable
  • AADT_COMBINATION: Heavy trucks, such as tractor-trailers
  • AADT_SINGLE_UNIT: Medium trucks, delivery vehicles, and buses

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Traffic volume is a core exposure measure because crash counts should be interpreted relative to the amount of traffic using the roadway.
Traffic volume is a core exposure measure because crash counts should be interpreted relative to the amount of traffic using the roadway.

4. Road Geometry

Lane counts and width variables.

Context

Roadway geometry determines how traffic flows and how road users interact.

Variables

  • TOTALTRAVELLANES: Total travel lane count
  • TOTALTRAVELLANEWIDTH: Total travel lane width, measured in feet
  • TOTALCROSSSECTIONWIDTH: Total roadway cross-section width, measured in feet
  • INBOUND / OUTBOUND lanes: Directional lane structure

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Higher speeds
  • More complex interactions
  • Increased crash risk

5. Parking Infrastructure

On-street parking variables.

Context

On-street parking changes traffic behavior and visibility conditions.

Variables

  • TOTALPARKINGLANES: Number of parking lanes
  • TOTALPARKINGLANEWIDTH: Parking lane width, measured in feet

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Obstructs visibility
  • Increases turning conflicts
  • Creates dooring risks

6. Bike Lanes and Buffers

Bike lanes and physical buffers.

Context

Bike infrastructure reflects multimodal design and affects safety for cyclists. Buffers provide physical separation between traffic modes.

Variables

  • TOTALBIKELANES: Number of bike lanes
  • TOTALBIKELANEWIDTH: Bike lane width, measured in feet
  • TOTALRAISEDBUFFERS: Number of raised buffers
  • TOTALRAISEDBUFFERWIDTH: Raised buffer width, measured in feet

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Supports cyclist safety
  • Improves mode separation
  • Reduces collision probability
  • Improves safety perception for vulnerable users

7. Pavement

PCI, IRI, and surface type measures.

Context

Road surface quality affects vehicle control, braking distance, and crash likelihood.

Variables

  • PCI_SCORE: Overall pavement condition rating from 0 to 100
  • IRI: Road surface roughness indicator, commonly measured in m/km
  • SURFACE_TYPE: Road pavement material type, such as asphalt, concrete, brick, or gravel

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Lower PCI may reduce vehicle control and increase braking distance
  • IRI coverage is incomplete because it is only collected on a subset of roads
  • Surface material may affect vehicle stability and crash risk

8. Traffic Calming

Vertical deflection features.

Context

Traffic calming measures are designed to reduce vehicle speed.

Variables

  • VERTICAL_DEFLECTION: Indicator for speed bumps, speed humps, raised tables, or similar features

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Reduces speed
  • May reduce crash severity

9. Sidewalks

Sidewalk width and material.

Context

Sidewalks represent pedestrian infrastructure and walkability.

Variables

  • SIDEWALK_IB_WIDTH: Inbound sidewalk width, measured in feet
  • SIDEWALK_OB_WIDTH: Outbound sidewalk width, measured in feet
  • SIDEWALK_IB_PAVTYPE: Inbound sidewalk pavement type
  • SIDEWALK_OB_PAVTYPE: Outbound sidewalk pavement type

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Pedestrian safety
  • Exposure to traffic
  • Accessibility

10. Bus Lanes

Inbound and outbound bus lane indicators.

Context

Bus lanes indicate transit-priority infrastructure. These variables are sparse and may underrepresent transit infrastructure.

Variables

  • BUSLANE_INBOUND: Inbound bus lane indicator
  • BUSLANE_OUTBOUND: Outbound bus lane indicator

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Changes lane allocation
  • Affects traffic flow
  • Introduces interactions with buses

11. Speed Limits

Speed limit variables.

Context

Speed is one of the strongest predictors of crash severity.

Variables

  • SPEEDLIMITS_OB: Outbound speed limit, measured in mph
  • SPEEDLIMITS_IB: Inbound speed limit; not available in this dataset

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Higher speeds increase injury severity
  • Higher speeds reduce reaction time

12. FHWA Functional Class

National roadway classification.

Context

Functional class describes the role of a road in the national transportation network.

Variables

CodeClassification
1Interstate
2Principal Arterial - Other Freeways or Expressways
3Principal Arterial - Other
4Minor Arterial
5Major Collector
6Minor Collector
7Local

Relevance to Safety / Modeling

  • Traffic volumes
  • Speeds
  • Risk profiles

13. DC Functional Class

Local DC roadway classification.

Context

DC functional classification provides local refinement of roadway hierarchy.

Variables

CodeClassification
11Interstate
12Other Freeway and Expressway
14Principal Arterial
16Minor Arterial
17Major Collector
18Minor Collector
19Local

14. Data Limitations and Coverage

Known limitations related to data completeness, coverage, and measurement methodology.

Context

This dataset contains several known limitations that should be considered when interpreting model results.

Units of Measurement

Variable TypeUnit
Length / width variablesFeet, except length_m which is measured in meters
Speed variablesMiles per hour (mph)
AADT variablesAverage daily vehicle counts

Additional Data Quality Notes

  • The SHAPELEN field is expected to represent segment length; however, all values appear as zero in this dataset. It is therefore dropped and replaced with length_m.
  • Turning lane variables, such as LEFTTURN_CURBLANE_EXCL and RIGHTTURN_EXCLUSIVE, are defined as the length of exclusive turn lanes approaching intersections, but these fields currently contain zero or missing values and are not used in analysis.
  • General data quality issues were observed in multiple fields with excessive zero values, indicating potential gaps in data population or system limitations.
  • Underlying data may still require additional QA/QC validation.

AADT Definitions

  • AADT: Total vehicle volume
  • AADT_COMBINATION: Combination trucks, such as tractor-trailers
  • AADT_SINGLE_UNIT: Single-unit trucks and buses
  • These values are derived from observed counts and modeled to the current year.

Temporal Coverage

The dataset is periodically aggregated and updated. The timestamp provided in Open Data should not be interpreted as the actual update date because it may not refresh automatically.

Roadway Segmentation

Roadway segments are based on block segmentation. Each segment begins at one street intersection and ends at the next. Only street-to-street intersections define segment boundaries.

Summary: SHAPELEN is excluded due to invalid zero values. length_m is the corrected roadway length measure used for aggregation, normalization, and tract-level comparability.
